summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses how DuckDB was built as a radical bet on single-node, in-process analytics over distributed scale-out architectures. Born from research at CWI Amsterdam, DuckDB combined state-of-the-art vectorized query execution with SQLite-like ease of use, after an earlier experiment (MonetDB Lite) proved the in-process model had demand. The 1.0 release focused on storage format stability and backward compatibility. Hannes explains how DuckDB's SQL dialect innovations like GROUP BY ALL have been widely copied, and discusses the emerging lakehouse ecosystem with mixed feelings about potential re-centralization of data access behind APIs.
key_points: DuckDB's fundamental bet was uncompromising single-node scale-up over distributed scale-out, which gives algorithmic advantages that distributed engines cannot match
The in-process deployment model eliminates server configuration, credentials, and maintenance overhead, making databases accessible like libraries rather than services
DuckDB has zero runtime dependencies and maintains compatibility with ancient Linux versions, trading cutting-edge API usage for universal portability
The community extension system localizes expertise and keeps the core lean while allowing ecosystem growth without dependency bloat
SQL ergonomic improvements like GROUP BY ALL emerged from the team actually using the database and noting pain points, and have been widely adopted by Snowflake and Databricks
money_quotes: The fundamental hill that we were willing to die on was to bet on single node and scale up instead of scale out.

What we set out to do with DuckDB is to somehow bring the orthodoxy of databases, features like transactions, like schemas, like state-of-the-art execution engines together with sort of this ease of use that we saw with systems like Mongo.
The whole field was chasing whales and the one customer in the world that has Google scale is Google.
You know what, let's just solve the problems for the 99% of people who are not Google and will be rich and turns out that worked.
Many DuckDB jobs now complete faster than a Spark cluster can start up.

If you compare things like Spark running on a single node versus DuckDB running on the same node, you typically see some like 10X difference in performance, not because we know more, but because we make assumptions that they cannot make.

We actually didn't tell anyone for the longest time. I think for almost a year, we didn't tell anyone what we were doing. We would show up to group meetings and say, yeah, we were working on query parsing.
How can we make something that people will actually use and not hate?
key_insight: By refusing to distribute and betting everything on single-node performance, DuckDB exploited algorithmic advantages unavailable to distributed systems and captured the 99% of users who never needed Google-scale infrastructure.
